column2:
1. Text normalization
2. Phonetic analysis (G2P)
3. Prosodic analysis
4. Acoustic model (mel spectrogram)
5. Vocoder (waveform)

Source: L08:pipeline, solution_ex3

column1: What is a diphone? Why preferred over phones?
column2:
A diphone spans half of each adjacent phone — captures coarticulation transitions. Sounds more natural than isolated phones.

Source: exam2024:Q6a-i

column1: Unit selection: target cost vs. join cost?
column2:
Target cost: how well a unit matches desired acoustic features.
Join cost: discontinuity at unit boundaries.
Minimize total cost to select best sequence.

Source: exam2024:Q6a-ii

column1: Tacotron 2 vs. FastSpeech — key architectural difference?
column2:
Tacotron 2: autoregressive decoder + attention (implicit duration).
FastSpeech: non-autoregressive + duration predictor (parallel decoding).

Source: exam2024:Q6b-ii, solution_ex3:2a

Griffin-Lim: iterative phase recovery, no training, fast but robotic.
WaveNet: autoregressive neural, high quality, slow.
HiFi-GAN: GAN vocoder, parallel, real-time, near WaveNet quality.

Source: exam2024:Q6b-iii, solution_ex3:3a

column1: DiffWave: forward process, reverse process, conditioning?
column2:
Forward: add Gaussian noise over steps.
Reverse: iteratively denoise, predict noise at each step.
Conditioned on: mel spectrogram.

Source: exam2025:Q6b-ii, solution_ex3:3d

column1: Why learned speaker embeddings over one-hot in multi-speaker TTS?
column2:
Embeddings capture continuous similarity between speakers, enable interpolation, and generalize better than sparse one-hot vectors.

Source: exam2025:Q6b-iii, solution_ex3:4a
